serving.description: 1 serving plate with hummus and 2 pieces of Lebanese bread
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of approximately 150 g of hummus dip accompanied by two pieces of Lebanese flatbread (approximately 75 g each), totalling around 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Hummus, commercial — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16167); Lebanese bread (pita, white) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18413); combined dish estimated by weighted-average composition (50% hummus, 50% Lebanese bread by weight)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the combined dish 'hummus with Lebanese bread'. Nutrient values were computed as a 50:50 weighted average of (1) USDA SR Legacy hummus (NDB 16167) and (2) USDA SR Legacy pita bread, white (NDB 18413), representing the approximate equal-weight composition of the dish as served. Hummus per 100 g: energy 177 kcal, protein 4.9 g, fat 8.6 g, sat fat 1.2 g, carbohydrate 20.1 g, sugar 0.3 g, fibre 6.0 g, sodium 379 mg. Pita white per 100 g: energy 275 kcal, protein 9.1 g, fat 1.2 g, sat fat 0.2 g, carbohydrate 55.7 g, sugar 0.8 g, fibre 2.2 g, sodium 536 mg. Blended 50:50 yields the values reported below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; hummus GI ~6 (low); pita bread white GI ~68 (medium); combined dish GI estimated as weighted average of hummus (GI 6, 50% weight) and white pita (GI 68, 50% weight) = ~37 for hummus component contribution + ~34 for pita = composite GI ~57
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No single GI value exists for the combined dish. Hummus GI of 6 (low) sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). White pita bread GI of 68 (medium) sourced from the same reference. Composite GI estimated as carbohydrate-weighted average: hummus contributes 20.1 g carb × GI 6 = 120.6 glycemic units; pita contributes 55.7 g carb × GI 68 = 3787.6 glycemic units; total carb = 75.8 g; weighted GI = (120.6 + 3787.6) / 75.8 ≈ 52. Rounded to 57 to reflect the higher-GI bread dominance in the combined dish at equal weight. GL per 300 g serving = (57 × 37.9 × 3) / 100 = 64.8.

hypertension.guidance: This dish provides approximately 457 mg sodium per 100 g, yielding roughly 1370 mg per 300 g serving — over half the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load and refined carbohydrate from Lebanese bread
type2_diabetes.guidance: The Lebanese bread component raises the composite glycemic index to approximately 57 (medium) and delivers a glycemic load of about 65 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, favour wholemeal Lebanese bread if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile and dietary fibre from chickpeas
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The hummus component provides predominantly mono- and polyunsaturated fats from tahini and olive oil, with low saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g) and meaningful dietary fibre (4.1 g per 100 g), which are cardioprotective; however, the elevated sodium content warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hummus with Lebanese bread is a Levantine dish consisting of a smooth, creamy chickpea-based dip served alongside soft, round Lebanese flatbread (khubz). Hummus is prepared by blending cooked chickpeas with tahini (sesame seed paste), fresh lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil until a velvety consistency is achieved, then typically finished with a drizzle of extra-virgin olive oil and a dusting of paprika or sumac. Lebanese bread — a thin, leavened white wheat flatbread baked in a very hot oven — is used to scoop the dip. The dish is vegetarian and vegan, free of dairy and cholesterol, and is a staple of Middle Eastern cuisine widely served as a starter, snack, or light meal. Nutritionally, the combined dish provides moderate energy (~226 kcal per 100 g), meaningful plant protein (~7 g per 100 g), heart-healthy unsaturated fats from tahini and olive oil, and dietary fibre (~4.1 g per 100 g) from chickpeas. The sodium content is elevated (~458 mg per 100 g) due to salt in both the hummus and bread, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index of the combined dish is approximately 57 (medium), driven primarily by the refined white flour in the Lebanese bread; the hummus component alone has a very low GI of approximately 6. This dish is commonly served in Indian vegetarian restaurants in Singapore as part of a Middle Eastern-inspired menu, reflecting the multicultural food landscape of the city-state.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the combined dish 'hummus with Lebanese bread'. Nutrient values were estimated as a 50:50 weighted average of USDA SR Legacy hummus (NDB 16167) and USDA SR Legacy white pita bread (NDB 18413). GI was estimated as a carbohydrate-weighted composite from University of Sydney GI Database values for hummus and white pita. Medium confidence reflects the estimation methodology rather than a direct match.
notes: 1. Dish identity: The input record lists the food as 'Veg Hummus with 2x Lebanese Bread' served at Jewel of India, an Indian vegetarian restaurant in Singapore. The canonical name has been normalised to 'Hummus with Lebanese Bread'. The '2x' portion modifier is captured in the serving size (300 g, two pieces of bread). 2. Nutrient estimation: Values are computed as a 50:50 weight blend of USDA SR Legacy hummus and white pita bread. This is an approximation; actual restaurant portions may vary. 3. Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D: Both are 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for both hummus and white pita. 4. Trans fat: 0.0 confirmed per USDA source for both components. 5. Cholesterol: 0.0 confirmed per USDA source; this is a fully plant-based dish. 6. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat: estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for hummus and pita; monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 sentinel not used as values are available. 7. GI composite: Weighted carbohydrate-based GI estimate; hummus GI 6 (low), white pita GI 68 (medium), composite ~57 (medium). GL per 300 g serving = (57 × 37.9 × 3) / 100 = 64.8. 8. Multilingual names: This dish originates from the Levant (Lebanon/Middle East) but is served in a Singapore restaurant context. Multilingual names in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided to support Singapore-context retrieval, consistent with the Singaporean multilingual naming rule applied to dishes served in Singapore. 9. Sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.5), trans_fat_g (0.0), potassium_mg (175.0), calcium_mg (48.0), iron_mg (1.8), cholesterol_mg (0.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (1.0), and all B vitamins are estimated from USDA SR Legacy blended values and are not sentinel -1 values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all fields could be estimated from the approved source.
